0:0.rendering: What was from the beginning, what we have heard, what we have seen with our eyes, what we have gazed upon and our hands have touched, concerning the word of life.
0:0.note: The Greek verbs for "seen" and "gazed upon" echo the language of eyewitness testimony in ancient courts, grounding this letter in tangible, physical encounter.

0:1.rendering: This life was revealed, and we have seen it. We testify to it and announce to you the eternal life that was with the Father and has been revealed to us.
0:1.note: The phrase "was with the Father" echoes John 1:1's Word who was with God, linking this letter back to the Gospel's opening.

0:2.rendering: what we have seen and heard, we also announce to you, so that you too may have fellowship with us, and our fellowship is with the Father and with his Son Jesus Christ.
0:2.note: The word "fellowship" echoes the shared life of Israel around the tabernacle, now extended through the apostles to a new community gathered around the incarnate Word.

0:3.rendering: We are writing these things so that our joy may be complete.
0:3.note: The word for "joy" echoes the same term in Jesus' farewell discourse (John 15:11), linking the letter's purpose to the shared life of the believing community.

0:4.rendering: This is the message we have heard from him and announce to you: that God is light, and in him there is no darkness at all.
0:4.note: The stark Hebrew-flavored contrast between light and darkness here echoes the creation account and prepares readers for the ethical call to walk in that same light.

0:5.rendering: If we say we have fellowship with him while we walk in the darkness, we lie and do not practice the truth.
0:5.note: The stark either-or here echoes the Gospel of John, where walking in light or darkness reveals whether our claim to know God is true or a self-deception.

0:6.rendering: But if we walk in the light, as he is in the light, we have fellowship with one another, and the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin.
0:6.note: The Greek for "walk" echoes the Old Testament idea of living in God's presence, where shared light creates true community and the blood that once marked doorposts now purifies the whole people.

0:7.rendering: If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us.
0:7.note: The Greek word for "deceive" here echoes the serpent's cunning in Genesis, exposing how self-deception blocks the light that Jesus brings.

1:0.rendering: My little children, I am writing these things to you so that you might not sin. But if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous one.
1:0.note: The word "advocate" is the same as the "Helper" promised in John's Gospel, a legal defender who stands beside us.

1:1.rendering: He himself is the atoning sacrifice for our sins, and not only for ours but also for the sins of the whole world.
1:1.note: The word "atoning sacrifice" echoes the mercy seat in the ancient tabernacle, where God met his people.

1:2.rendering: And by this we know that we have come to know him: if we keep his commands.
1:2.note: The Greek for "know" (ginosko) here echoes the intimate relational knowing of covenant in the Old Testament, turning mere information into lived obedience.

1:3.rendering: The one who says, "I have come to know him," yet does not keep his commands, is a liar, and the truth is not in him.
1:3.note: This verse echoes Jesus' own words in the Gospel of John that loving him means keeping his commands, exposing a disconnect between claim and life.

1:4.rendering: But whoever keeps his word, truly in this person the love of God has been perfected. By this we know that we are in him.
1:4.note: The phrase "keeps his word" echoes the Shema's call to love God by guarding his commands, now fulfilled in those who abide in the Messiah.

1:5.rendering: The one who says he remains in him ought to walk just as that one walked.
1:5.note: The Greek for "remain" echoes Jesus' vine-and-branches image in John 15, turning a claim of intimacy into a call to mirror his concrete way of life.

1:6.rendering: Beloved, I am not writing you a new commandment, but an old one that you have had from the beginning. The old commandment is the word that you heard.
1:6.note: This echoes Deuteronomy 6:4-6 and Leviticus 19:18, the ancient command to love that Israel heard at Sinai, now reframed as the familiar word they already possess.

1:7.rendering: Yet I am writing you a new command, one that is true in him and in you, because the darkness is passing away and the true light is already shining.
1:7.note: This "new" command echoes Jesus' words in John 13:34 yet loops back to the ancient command of Leviticus 19:18, showing how love's light is both old and dawning new.

0:8.rendering: If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just and will forgive us our sins and cleanse us from all unrighteousness.
0:8.note: The Greek word for "confess" means to say the same thing about our sins that God does, aligning our view with his.

0:9.rendering: If we say, "We have not sinned," we make him a liar, and his word is not in us.
0:9.note: This verse echoes the proverb that "the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom" by warning that claiming sinlessness is self-deception that blocks God's truth from shaping us.

1:8.rendering: The one who says, "I am in the light," and yet hates his brother, is still in the darkness even now.
1:8.note: The stark contrast between claiming light and harboring hate echoes the ancient idea that true fellowship with God must show itself in love for the community, not just private piety.

1:9.rendering: The one who loves his brother remains in the light, and there is no cause for stumbling in him.
1:9.note: The Greek word for "stumbling" (skandalon) echoes Jesus' warning in Matthew 18 about becoming a stumbling block, showing love keeps us from tripping others in the dark.

1:10.rendering: But the one who hates his brother is in the darkness, and walks in the darkness. He does not know where he is going, because the darkness has blinded his eyes.
1:10.note: The word "walks" echoes the Hebrew idiom for daily life from the Psalms and Proverbs, showing hatred as a path that literally blinds.

1:11.rendering: I am writing to you, little children, because your sins have been forgiven on account of his name.
1:11.note: The phrase "on account of his name" echoes the ancient practice of forgiveness tied to invoking God's covenant name, pointing to the unified biblical theme of deliverance through a representative figure.

1:12.rendering: I am writing to you, fathers, because you have known the one from the beginning. I am writing to you, young men, because you have overcome the evil one. I am writing to you, little children, because you have known the Father.
1:12.note: The three age groups echo Israel's journey from patriarchs to warriors to a new generation, all now united in knowing both the Ancient One and the Father through Jesus.

1:13.rendering: I have written to you, fathers, because you have known the one from the beginning. I have written to you, young men, because you are strong, and the word of God remains in you, and you have conquered the evil one.
1:13.note: The phrase "you have known the one from the beginning" echoes the opening of John's Gospel, linking mature believers to the eternal Word who was with the Father.

1:14.rendering: Do not love the world or the things in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him.
1:14.note: John echoes Jesus' warning that no one can serve two masters, using the same Greek word for "love" that Jesus used for loving God versus money.

1:15.rendering: For everything in the world, the craving of the flesh, the craving of the eyes, and the arrogance of life, comes not from the Father but from the world.
1:15.note: The three cravings echo the serpent's temptations in Eden, the forbidden fruit's appeal to appetite, sight, and wisdom.

1:16.rendering: The world and its desires are passing away, but the one who does God's will remains forever.
1:16.note: This verse echoes the Hebrew idea of "passing away" like fading grass in Isaiah 40:6-8, contrasting transient human glory with the enduring word and will of God.

1:17.rendering: Children, it is the last hour. And just as you heard that antichrist is coming, even now many antichrists have arisen. By this we know that it is the last hour.
1:17.note: The Greek "eschate hora" (last hour) echoes Jesus' own urgent warnings about the end-time distress, but John flips the script: the many antichrists already here prove the clock is striking.

1:18.rendering: They went out from us, but they were not of us, for if they had been of us, they would have remained with us. But their going out made it plain that none of them were of us.
1:18.note: The wordplay on "of us" and "from us" highlights a deep communal identity that cannot be faked, echoing the ancient idea that true belonging shows itself in staying power.

1:19.rendering: Yet you have an anointing from the Holy One, and you all know.
1:19.note: The word "anointing" (chrisma) echoes the title "Christ" (Christos), reminding these believers they share in the same Holy Spirit that rests on Jesus.

1:20.rendering: I have not written to you because you do not know the truth, but because you do know it, and because no lie comes from the truth.
1:20.note: The Greek wordplay on "know" (oida) and "truth" (aletheia) echoes John's Gospel, training the community to test every claim against the reality they already possess in Jesus.

1:21.rendering: Who is the liar if not the one who denies that Jesus is the Christ? This one is the antichrist, the one who denies the Father and the Son.
1:21.note: The Greek wordplay ties "liar" (pseustes) directly to the ultimate "antichrist" who severs the Father from the Son, echoing the Gospel's insistence that knowing one means knowing the other.

1:22.rendering: No one who denies the Son has the Father. The one who confesses the Son has the Father also.
1:22.note: This verse plays on the ancient Jewish idea of "having" God, echoing the intimate covenant language of the Old Testament where knowing the Father and Son are inseparably linked.

1:23.rendering: As for you, what you heard from the beginning, let it remain in you. If what you heard from the beginning remains in you, you also will remain in the Son and in the Father.
1:23.note: The Greek word "meno" (remain/abide) echoes Jesus' vine-and-branches teaching in John 15, linking perseverance to intimate connection with both Father and Son.

1:24.rendering: And this is the promise that he himself promised us: eternal life.
1:24.note: John deliberately repeats the word "promise" (epangelia) here, echoing the covenant language of the Old Testament where God repeatedly pledges life to his people.

1:25.rendering: I am writing these things to you about the ones who are trying to deceive you.
1:25.note: John's urgent warning echoes Jesus' own words about false christs and prophets who would deceive even the elect.

1:26.rendering: As for you, the anointing you received from him remains in you, and you have no need for anyone to teach you. But as his anointing teaches you about all things, and is true and is no lie, just as it has taught you, remain in him.
1:26.note: The Greek word for anointing, chrisma, echoes the Messiah's own title, suggesting the Spirit himself is the true teacher who makes every believer a little christos.

1:27.rendering: And now, little children, remain in him, so that when he appears we may have boldness and not shrink away from him in shame at his coming.
1:27.note: The Greek word for "remain" echoes Jesus' vine-and-branches image in John 15, inviting us to picture ourselves as branches that stay connected until the moment the vine-owner returns.

1:28.rendering: If you know that he is righteous, you know that everyone who does righteousness has been born from him.
1:28.note: The Greek wordplay links "righteous" (dikaios) directly to "does righteousness" (dikaiosynen), showing that true birth from God reshapes character to mirror his own.
